template_description: Social-ecological system template for coastal lagoon ecosystems, covering eutrophication, fisheries, habitat change, and management responses. Applicable to Baltic and Mediterranean lagoon systems.

connections.description: Intensified farming increases fertiliser use and drainage volumes flowing into the lagoon
Drainage channels deliver nitrogen and phosphorus directly to the lagoon, driving eutrophication

Excess nutrients, especially phosphorus, fuel cyanobacteria blooms in warm shallow lagoons. Nutrient loading (particularly nitrogen and phosphorus from agricultural runoff and municipal wastewater) drives phytoplankton and cyanobacterial bloom formation
Nutrient loading increases phytoplankton turbidity, shading out submerged macrophytes

Dense cyanobacteria blooms drastically reduce water clarity in shallow lagoons. The negative feedback reflects a trade-off or antagonistic interaction where increases in the source variable suppress or reduce the target through competitive exclusion, resource limitation, or regulatory mechanisms

Poor flushing allows nutrient accumulation and dominance by bloom-forming species. Nutrient loading (particularly nitrogen and phosphorus from agricultural runoff and municipal wastewater) drives phytoplankton and cyanobacterial bloom formation
Pike-perch population dynamics affects Fish provisioning from lagoon fisheries
Extensive reed beds provide essential nesting and staging habitat for waterbirds

Reed beds filter nutrients and trap sediment, improving water quality. The positive feedback operates through direct resource dependency, where changes in the source variable proportionally affect the target variable through established ecological and socioeconomic pathways

Fish catches directly translate to fisher income in lagoon communities. The positive feedback operates through direct resource dependency, where changes in the source variable proportionally affect the target variable through established ecological and socioeconomic pathways
Rich bird populations attract birdwatching tourists and generate local revenue. Marine tourism generates substantial economic value — reef tourism alone contributes ~USD 36 billion/year globally
International nutrient targets drive national measures that reduce agricultural nutrient loads
Buffer zones intercept nutrients before they reach drainage channels flowing to the lagoon

Minimum size limits reduce fishing pressure on juvenile pike-perch. The negative feedback reflects a trade-off or antagonistic interaction where increases in the source variable suppress or reduce the target through competitive exclusion, resource limitation, or regulatory mechanisms
Local food security needs directly drive small-scale gillnet fishing effort targeting pike-perch in lagoons
Natura 2000 conservation obligations impose restrictions on agricultural drainage practices affecting lagoon habitats
Progressive sedimentation narrows and shallows lagoon inlets, further restricting water exchange with the open sea and exacerbating stagnation
Submerged macrophytes stabilise sediments and reduce resuspension, maintaining clear water; macrophyte loss triggers a turbid-water stable state through positive feedback with phytoplankton dominance
Shift from diatom to cyanobacteria dominance reduces zooplankton food quality, impacting pike-perch larval survival through trophic cascade effects on prey availability
Small-scale gillnet fishing for pike-perch affects Fishing mortality on pike-perch stock
Fishing mortality on pike-perch stock affects Pike-perch population dynamics

Reed harvesting removes nutrient-rich biomass from the lagoon ecosystem, potentially reducing (not increasing) nutrient levels. Reed beds sequester N and P; their harvest exports these nutrients. Polarity corrected from + to -.
Eel fishing with fyke nets is a passive, low-impact gear that does not contribute meaningfully to nutrient enrichment. No scientific basis for medium-strength link. Strength and confidence reduced.

Microplastic effects on pike-perch populations are poorly documented and likely negative (not positive) through ingestion, inflammation, and endocrine disruption. Limited evidence for population-level effects. Polarity corrected to -, strength to weak, confidence to 2.
Best management practices directly reduce nutrient discharge from lagoon catchment activities, supporting HELCOM targets
Constructed wetland buffers intercept nitrogen and phosphorus before entering lagoon, supporting HELCOM nutrient reduction targets
Fisher household income from pike-perch sales reinforces Food security from local fisheries
Recreational value for local communities reinforces Recreational demand for nature tourism
Cultural identity of lagoon fishing villages reinforces Cultural heritage of traditional fishing
Tourism revenue from birdwatching and angling reinforces recreational demand for nature tourism
